rationale: Empirically verified on this machine (unlocked): CGSSessionScreenIsLocked absent, kCGSSessionOnConsoleKey=1 present — confirming the bug and validating A's and B's premise; genuinely-locked adds the key as true (checked first, short-circuits), so the absent-key branch only fires when the key is omitted. A (pure flip: absent→unlocked) fixes the primary case but bare-infers "absent = unlocked" with no corroboration — it reports UNLOCKED for a fast-user-switched-away / backgrounded session (key absent AND on-console false), which under the DEFAULT blockWhileLocked posture is a real false-unlocked (operator not present at console). Given the recorded concurrent-session/shared-agent race on this environment, that off-console case is realistic, not hypothetical. B closes exactly that gap by gating absent-key→unlocked on kCGSSessionOnConsoleKey==true, reusing the existing Bool/NSNumber coercion, for ~10 extra LOC — it is strictly more conservative than A (only ever adds fail-closed cases: switched-away, off-console remote, no-console) and never more permissive, directly serving hard-constraint 3. It also answers A's own open question ("is a corroborating second signal worth it") with a low-cost yes. Both A and B keep fail-closed for nil/empty/unparseable, leave the opt-in unchanged, and preserve the protocol/fakes API. C is a clear reject on KISS/YAGNI: 150-220 LOC, a new NotificationObserving abstraction, run-loop dependency (the dispatcher may run with no run loop, silently degrading to A/B anyway), and it INTRODUCES a new false-unlocked path via stale/missed distributed notifications — its own author recommends against it. Caveat driving the choice: B's console gate does not defend against a purely hypothetical future OS that omits the key WHILE locked (on-console would still be 1 during lock), so its real value is the switched-away/off-console distinction, not that theoretical case; that case is bounded instead by the retained rawKeysSeen diagnostic and the unchanged opt-in escape hatch. Correctness beyond macOS 26 is unverified for both — the fix is confidently correct only where empirically confirmed, so the false file-top "verified on macOS 12-15" claim must be corrected, not restated.
mustIncorporate: Extract an internal-static seam parseSessionDict(_ dict:[String:Any]?, rawKeys:Set<String>) -> MacSessionSnapshot (shared A/B recommendation) so fetchFromSystem stays a thin CGSessionCopyCurrentDictionary wrapper delegating to it; enables @testable unit coverage without touching the protocol or fakes.

Ordering: check CGSSessionScreenIsLocked PRESENT branch first (Bool/NSNumber true->locked, false->unlocked, unparseable->fail-closed isUnknown=true) and only fall through to the absent-key + on-console gate — an explicit lock signal must never be overridden by the console gate.

Console gate uses the exact literal key string 'kCGSSessionOnConsoleKey' (confirmed present in the live dict), coerced via the SAME Bool/NSNumber helper; absent-key + on-console==true -> (isLocked:false, isUnknown:false); absent-key + on-console false/absent/unparseable -> (isLocked:true, isUnknown:true) fail-closed.
Keep fail-closed unchanged for nil/empty dict and for unparseable CGSSessionScreenIsLocked values (hard-constraint 1); leave OPEN_COMPUTER_USE_ALLOW_LOCKED opt-in fully untouched (hard-constraint 2).
Preserve the existing DEBUG-only OPEN_COMPUTER_USE_LOCK_FAIL_OPEN override (lines ~102-106) when relocating logic into the seam — do not drop it during extraction.

Preserve the rawKeysSeen diagnostic and the existing requireUnlocked stderr logging so a future wrong-direction OS (locked-but-key-absent) is catchable from logs rather than failing silently (A's honest-weakness mitigation).

Correct the false file-top comment: state the absent-key=unlocked + on-console semantics were verified only on macOS 26 (Darwin 27) on this machine; downgrade/remove the unverified 'verified on macOS 12-15' claim rather than reasserting it.

Regression tests via @testable import covering the full table: nil dict->fail-closed; empty dict->fail-closed; key absent + on-console true->UNLOCKED (the exact bug regression guard); key absent + on-console false->fail-closed; key absent + on-console absent->fail-closed; key absent + on-console unparseable->fail-closed; key present true->locked (proves console gate never overrides an explicit lock signal); key present false->unlocked; key present unparseable->fail-closed.
Confirm all existing lock-policy tests remain green untouched — fakes implement MacSessionStateProvider directly and bypass fetchFromSystem/parseSessionDict, so protocol and fakes stay unchanged.

Reject C entirely: no DistributedNotificationCenter observer, no new abstraction, no run-loop-dependent signal — it adds a new stale-notification false-unlocked path and cost with unproven benefit over the dict-only fix.

Update docs (README.md ~42-47, ARCHITECTURE/RELIABILITY where the 'blocked while locked' framing and macOS-version claim appear) to reflect that unlocked-by-default now works and to drop the false macOS 12-15 verification claim.
rejectedSummary: A rejected as primary: correct minimal flip but bare-infers absent=unlocked with no corroboration, yielding a real false-unlocked for fast-user-switched-away/off-console sessions under default blockWhileLocked — B is strictly more conservative for ~10 extra LOC and answers A's own open question; A's good parts (seam, comment correction, regression cases, rawKeysSeen logging) are folded into B. C rejected outright on KISS/YAGNI: 150-220 LOC, new abstraction, run-loop dependency that silently degrades to A/B, and a new stale/missed-notification false-unlocked attack surface — author self-recommends against it.
